text:
Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta offers a diverse array of B.Tech programmes designed to cater to various engineering interests and technological advancements. Established since 1999, the Mechanical Engineering programme has been a cornerstone with an intake of 120 students annually. The Computer Science & Engineering (CSE) programme, initiated in 2001, boasts a larger intake of 180 students, reflecting its growing importance in the tech industry. Electrical and Civil Engineering programmes were introduced later, in 2004 and 2010 respectively, with intakes of 120 and 60 students each, addressing the increasing demand for skilled professionals in these fields.

In recent years, the college has expanded its offerings to include more specialized tracks. The Aeronautical Engineering programme, launched in 2013, caters to a niche but critical area with an intake of 60 students. Food Technology, introduced in 2019, addresses the growing need for professionals in food science and technology, also with 30 seats available.

The college has embraced emerging technologies by introducing newer specializations such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Data Science, which began in 2021 with an intake of 60 students. Similarly, Computer Science & Engineering with a focus on Internet of Things (IoT), Cyber Security including Blockchain Technology, also started in the same year, providing students with cutting-edge knowledge in these areas. The Robotics And Artificial Intelligence programme, launched for the academic year 2025-26, is expected to attract 60 students and will likely be a significant draw due to its innovative focus.

Additionally, the college offers Bachelor of Business Administration (BBA) and Bachelor of Computer Application (BCA), both introduced in 2024 with intakes of 60 students each. These programmes aim to bridge the gap between technology and business management, preparing students for a dynamic job market that requires both technical skills and business acumen.

Together, these programmes cover a broad spectrum of engineering disciplines, from traditional fields like Mechanical and Civil Engineering to emerging areas such as AI, IoT, and Robotics, ensuring a well-rounded education for future engineers.

text:
For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, prospective students will find a clear tuition fee structure based on their reservation category for both First Year (FY) and Direct Second Year (DSE) admissions.

The OPEN category, which includes students from general categories with no reservations, is charged an annual tuition fee of Rs. 134,026 for FY and DSE admissions. This fee remains the same for both entry points, indicating a uniform cost structure for non-reserved category students.

For those belonging to the EBC (Economically Backward Class), EWS (Economically Weaker Section), OBC (Other Backward Classes), SBC (Scheduled Backward Classes), VJNT (Very Poor and Destitute Tribal Nationalities), and TFWS (Tuition Fee Waiver Scheme) categories, the fee is significantly lower. The FY tuition for these categories stands at Rs. 82,505, while DSE admissions cost Rs. 82,505 as well.

The SC (Scheduled Castes) and ST (Scheduled Tribes) categories have the lowest fees among all reservation categories. For both FY and DSE admissions, students from these categories are charged a tuition fee of only Rs. 15,526 per annum. This substantial reduction in cost is aimed at providing equal educational opportunities to marginalized communities.

The fee structure ranges from the highest for OPEN category students at Rs. 134,026, down to SC/ST students who pay the lowest at Rs. 15,526. The DSE admissions follow a similar pattern with slight variations in fees based on the reservation categories. This transparent and categorized fee structure ensures that all students can access quality education regardless of their socio-economic background.

Moreover, the convenience of having meals provided in the hostel can be particularly advantageous, allowing students to save time and energy typically spent on daily commutes or meal planning.

- Route 'Ashta-Miraj' with 6 stops, catering to the north-eastern part.
- Route 'Ashta-Shirala' also with 7 stops, covering the south-eastern area.
- Route 'Ashta-Kolhapur' with 7 stops, extending towards the southern region.
- Route 'Ashta-Dudhondi' with 7 stops, serving the north-western part.
- Route 'Ashta-Tasgaon' with 7 stops, reaching further west.
- Route 'Ashta-Karad' with 6 stops, covering the south-western area.

This network ensures that students can travel to and from various nearby towns and villages. The monthly bus fare is structured on a distance-based system, meaning the cost varies according to the route length and number of stops covered. This fee structure aims to provide affordable transportation while maintaining operational efficiency for the college's transport services.

text:
For the Mechanical Engineering (Mech) branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26, the MHT-CET cutoff marks provide a clear indication of the minimum merit required by students from different reservation categories to secure admission. The terms 'G' and 'L' refer to General seats and Ladies seats, respectively.

The VJ category (Vidarbha Jyotirao Phule) has distinct cutoffs for both General and Ladies seats at 50.70 marks and 44.26 marks, respectively. This means that a student from the VJ category must secure at least 50.70 marks in their MHT-CET to be eligible for a General seat.

NT-1 (Nagpur) has higher cutoffs compared to VJ, with General and Ladies seats requiring 71.66 marks and 40.68 marks respectively. This indicates that the competition is stiffer for students from this category.

For NT-2, another Nagpur-based category, the cutoffs are slightly lower at 71.48 marks for General and 70.62 marks for Ladies seats. The slight difference in scores between these two categories suggests a nuanced approach to reservation within the same region.

NT-3 has notably lower cutoffs with only 25.21 marks required for General seats, though there are no specific marks mentioned for Ladies seats, implying that all eligible candidates from this category may be considered based on their MHT-CET scores.

The OBC (Other Backward Classes) and SEBC (Specially Educated Backward Classes) categories have higher cutoffs of 65.31 and 75.40 marks for General seats, respectively, with corresponding Ladies seats at 68.47 and 73.13 marks. These higher scores reflect the competitive nature of these categories.

The OPEN category has the highest cutoffs among all with 76.93 marks required for General seats and 75.40 marks for Ladies seats, indicating that students from this category must perform exceptionally well in their MHT-CET to secure a seat.

For SC (Scheduled Castes) and ST (Scheduled Tribes), the cutoffs are significantly lower at 40.68 and N/A respectively for General seats, with corresponding lower scores of 36.23 and N/A marks for Ladies seats. This reflects the affirmative action policies aimed at ensuring equal opportunities.

DEF (Defence Forces) category has a higher cutoff of 52.36 marks for General seats but no specific score is mentioned for Ladies seats, suggesting that all eligible candidates may be considered based on their MHT-CET scores.

Lastly, TFWS (Tribal Welfare Scheme) and EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) categories have the highest cutoffs at 83.11 marks for General seats and 28.98 marks for Ladies seats, respectively, indicating that these categories are reserved for students with the best MHT-CET scores.

In summary, each reservation category has specific merit requirements, which vary based on the category and gender of the candidate. Students should carefully review their eligibility under different categories to maximize their chances of securing admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology's Mechanical Engineering program for 2025-26.

For the academic year 2025-26 at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta, the cutoff marks for the ELECT (Electronics and Communication) branch under MHT-CET are as follows:

The 'VJ' category, which stands for Very Jat, has a general seat cutoff of 53.76 marks and a ladies seat cutoff of 64.39 marks. The 'NT-1' category, or Non-Tribe - I, requires a minimum of 66.17 marks for the general seat, with no specific mark mentioned for the ladies seat.

For 'NT-2', another non-tribe category, the cutoff is significantly higher at 73.75 marks for the general seat and 78.01 marks for the ladies seat. The 'OBC' (Other Backward Classes) category has a general seat cutoff of 70.62 marks and 76.90 marks for the ladies seat, while the 'SEBC' (Socio-Economically Backward Classes) category requires 76.93 marks for the general seat and 77.16 marks for the ladies.

The 'OPEN' category, which is reserved for non-reserved students, has a higher cutoff of 77.95 marks for the general seat and an even higher 81.97 marks for the ladies seat. For 'SC' (Scheduled Castes) candidates, the required merit marks are lower at 49.73 marks for the general seat and 55.64 marks for the ladies seat.

The 'ST' category (Scheduled Tribes) does not have specific cutoffs mentioned in this data; hence, it is unclear if there were any applicants from this category or if no marks are applicable due to a lack of candidates. The 'DEF' category, which stands for Defence Forces, has a general seat cutoff of 54.59 marks and no ladies seat mark specified.

Lastly, the 'TFWS' (Tribal Welfare Scheme) category requires 82.97 marks for the general seat, with no specific mark mentioned for the ladies seat. The 'EWS' (Economically Weaker Sections) category has a lower cutoff of 45.36 marks for the general seat and no specific mark for the ladies seat.

These cutoffs indicate that students from different reservation categories have varying merit requirements to secure admission into the ELECT branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology, with 'OPEN' and 'SC' having notably lower thresholds compared to other categories.

text:
For the CSE IOT branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26, the MHT-CET (Maharashtra Common Entrance Test) cutoff marks provide a clear indication of the minimum merit required to secure admission. The terms 'G' and 'L' denote General seats and Ladies seats respectively.

The VJ category has a general seat cutoff of 69.22 marks, which is the minimum score needed for male students in this category. No specific cutoff is mentioned for ladies from this category; hence, if a female student applies under VJ, her performance will be compared against other candidates irrespective of gender.

In NT-1 and NT-3 categories, no general seat cutoffs are provided, meaning that the minimum marks required to secure a general seat have not been set. However, for NT-2, the general seat requirement is 81.80 marks, while for ladies it is slightly lower at 79.30 marks. For NT-3, the cutoff for ladies is 41.45 marks.

The OBC category requires a minimum of 79.70 marks for general seats and 81.01 marks for ladies to secure admission. SEBC (Specially Educated Backward Class) students need to score at least 82.97 marks in the general seat category, with a higher requirement of 84.64 marks for ladies.

For OPEN category applicants, both general and ladies seats require a minimum of 83.07 marks. SC (Scheduled Caste) candidates must achieve at least 64.71 marks for general seats and 68.47 marks for ladies to be considered for admission. ST (Scheduled Tribe) students do not have any specified cutoffs, neither for general nor ladies categories.

DEF (Defence Forces) category requires a minimum of 71.17 marks in the general seat category, with no specific mention for ladies. TFWS (Tribal Female Welfare Scheme) has a high requirement of 83.88 marks for both general and ladies seats. Lastly, EWS (Economically Weaker Sections) candidates need to secure at least 52.60 marks in the general seat category.

These cutoffs indicate that students must perform well in their MHT-CET to be eligible for admission into the CSE IOT branch. Each reservation category has its own set of requirements, and it is crucial for applicants to understand these criteria to assess their eligibility accurately.

text:
The Mechanical Engineering branch at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology, Ashta, has seen a steady improvement in its placement outcomes over the past three years. In 2023-24, with 112 students participating, there were 105 offers, yielding an average package of 4.5 LPA, with the highest package reaching 33 LPA. Moving to 2024-25, despite a slight decrease in student numbers to 87, the college witnessed a significant increase in offer count to 132, leading to an improved average package of 4.7 LPA and a record-breaking highest package of 6.5 LPA. The following year, 2025-26, saw a modest rise in student numbers back to 96, with 122 offers being made; this resulted in an average package of 5 LPA, and the highest package soared to 8.5 LPA.

These trends suggest that industry demand for Mechanical Engineering graduates from Annasaheb Dange College is on a positive trajectory. The increase in offer count indicates growing interest from employers, while the rise in average and highest packages reflects both an improvement in overall employability and a higher value placed on the skills offered by this branch. The data also suggests that there might be specific sectors or companies showing increased demand for Mechanical Engineering talent, possibly due to advancements in technology or changes in market dynamics. Overall, these trends are encouraging signs of robust industry engagement with the college's Mechanical Engineering program.

text:
The placement season at Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology (ADCOET) for the academic year 2025-26 was a notable success across various engineering branches, reflecting a robust demand from industry for graduates.

In Mechanical Engineering, 96 students secured placements with an average package of 5 LPA. The highest package offered in this branch was 8.5 LPA, indicating strong performance and competitiveness among the candidates. This branch saw 122 total offers, which is significantly higher than other branches, reflecting a high demand for mechanical engineers.

Civil Engineering had 34 students placed with an average package of 5 LPA, and the highest offer was 6.5 LPA. The number of placements here was lower compared to Mechanical Engineering but still represented a solid performance in terms of job opportunities secured by graduates.

Electrical Engineering boasted the largest number of students at 153, with 171 offers and an average package of 5 LPA, with the highest being 6.5 LPA. This branch demonstrated strong placement numbers, indicating a high level of interest from employers in electrical engineering talent.

Computer Science Engineering had 145 students placed, securing 120 offers on average at 5.2 LPA, with the standout highest package reaching an impressive 12.34 LPA. This branch not only showed a significant number of placements but also highlighted the high potential for lucrative job opportunities in the tech sector.

Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering had 69 students placed with 41 offers and an average package of 5.2 LPA, with the highest being 8.67 LPA. This branch demonstrated a growing demand from employers, especially given the increasing importance of cybersecurity skills in today's digital landscape.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering had 73 students placed with 43 offers at an average package of 5.2 LPA, with the highest being 7.5 LPA. This relatively new branch showed promising results, indicating a strong interest from industry in graduates skilled in AI and data science.

Aeronautical Engineering saw 48 students placed with 33 offers and an average package of 4.6 LPA, with the highest at 6 LPA. While this number is lower compared to some other branches, it still represents a significant achievement for the students involved.

Food Technology had only 8 students placed but secured 21 offers, averaging 4 LPA with the highest being 5 LPA. This branch's performance was commendable despite the smaller student body, showing that there are opportunities in niche fields as well.

Overall, ADCOET recorded a total of 588 placements from 105 companies visited during the placement season for 2025-26. The Computer Science Engineering branch emerged as the top-performing branch with the highest number of students placed and the highest package offered at 12.34 LPA, making it a standout performer in this academic year.

These results reflect not only the quality of education provided by ADCOET but also the strong industry engagement that ensures graduates are well-prepared for their future careers.

text:
The placement season for Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology (ADCET) in Ashta for the academic year 2024-25 was marked by a robust performance across various engineering branches, showcasing the college's commitment to providing quality education and ensuring its students are well-prepared for their careers.

In Mechanical Engineering, 87 students secured job offers, with an average package of 4.7 LPA (Lakhs Per Annum), and the highest package reached 6.5 LPA. This branch demonstrated a strong performance, reflecting the industry's continued interest in mechanical engineering graduates.

Civil Engineering saw 70 students receive offers, averaging 4.8 LPA with the highest package at 7.5 LPA. The average was slightly higher than that of Mechanical Engineering, indicating a competitive job market for civil engineers from ADCET.

Electrical Engineering had the largest number of participants with 146 students, and it also boasted the most offers with 127, averaging 5.2 LPA and reaching up to 9 LPA. This branch stood out as one of the top performers in terms of both offer count and average package.

Computer Science Engineering had a significant number of participants at 131 students, but only 90 received offers, with an average package of 5.2 LPA and a highest package of 8 LPA. Despite fewer offers compared to Electrical Engineering, the packages were competitive, highlighting the high demand for computer science graduates.

Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering had 62 participants, securing 40 offers, averaging 5.2 LPA with a top package of 7.5 LPA. This branch showed strong performance in terms of average and highest packages, indicating its relevance in today's tech-driven job market.

Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering saw 70 students, receiving 47 offers, with an average package of 5.2 LPA and a top package of 6 LPA. This branch also performed well, reflecting the growing importance of AI and data science skills among employers.

Aeronautical Engineering had fewer participants at 33 students, but it secured 28 offers, averaging 4.6 LPA with the highest at 4.5 LPA. While the numbers were lower compared to other branches, this branch still managed to secure competitive packages for its students.

Food Technology was the smallest branch with only 21 participants, securing 17 offers, averaging 3.5 LPA and reaching a maximum of 4 LPA. This branch's performance was notable given its smaller size but still provided valuable job opportunities for its students.

Overall, ADCET recorded a total of 511* offers till date from 90+ companies visited. The Electrical Engineering branch emerged as the top performer in terms of both offer count and average package, with an impressive 127 offers averaging at 5.2 LPA and reaching up to 9 LPA. This success is a testament to the college's curriculum and industry partnerships.

The highest individual packages across all branches were from Electrical Engineering, with the top package being 9 LPA. The strong performance in this branch underscores its relevance and demand in the current job market.

text:
The placement season for Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology (ADCET) in Ashta during the academic year 2023-24 was marked by a robust performance across various engineering branches. The college managed to secure a total of 554 job offers from 82 companies, reflecting a strong demand for its graduating engineers.

In terms of package sizes, Computer Science Engineering (CSE) emerged as the top-performing branch with an average salary of ₹5 LPA and a highest package of ₹10 LPA. This was followed closely by Electrical Engineering (EE), which offered an average package of ₹4.8 LPA and a peak offer of ₹9 LPA. Mechanical Engineering (ME) also performed well, achieving an average package of ₹4.5 LPA with the highest package being ₹33 LPA.

The Civil Engineering branch saw 73 students receive offers at an average salary of ₹4.5 LPA, but it's worth noting that the highest package was only ₹6 LPA. Aeronautical Engineering (AE) had a slightly lower average package of ₹4.3 LPA with a highest offer of ₹5.5 LPA, and Food Technology (FT) offered an average salary of ₹3.8 LPA, with the best package being ₹4.5 LPA.

Notably, two newer branches—Computer Science IoT & Cybersecurity Engineering (CS-ICSE) and Artificial Intelligence and Data Science Engineering (AI-DSE)—did not have any students securing job offers this year. This could be due to various factors such as a smaller number of graduating students or the relatively new nature of these programs.

In terms of offer counts, Mechanical Engineering led with 105 offers for its 112 students, followed by Electrical Engineering with 117 offers out of 145 applicants. Aeronautical Engineering and Food Technology each had a 100% placement rate, offering all 55 and 36 eligible students respectively.

Computer Science Engineering had the highest number of placements at 142 out of 153 students, showcasing its strong demand in the job market. This was closely followed by Electrical Engineering with 99 offers from 145 applicants.

Overall, ADCET's placement season was successful, with a total of 554 job offers across all branches. The highest package achieved this year was ₹33 LPA in Mechanical Engineering, which is an impressive figure and highlights the strong performance of that branch. The college’s diverse range of engineering disciplines contributed to its overall success, with Computer Science Engineering leading both in terms of offer count and average salary.

The placement season at ADCET demonstrates a healthy job market for engineering graduates from this institution, with several branches achieving notable results. While some newer programs did not secure any placements, the majority of students found employment opportunities that aligned well with their educational background.

text:
For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in the academic year 2025-26 for B.Tech., BBA, or BCA programs, students must submit a comprehensive set of documents. The MHT-CET or JEE 2026 Marksheet is essential as it verifies the candidate's eligibility based on entrance exam scores. The 10th and 12th Marksheet are required to confirm educational qualifications, while the Diploma Marksheet (if applicable) ensures that candidates meet the necessary academic standards.

The Leaving / Transfer Certificate serves to validate a student’s current status or transfer from another institution. Domicile / Nationality Certificates, AADHAR Card with linked mobile number, and Migration Certificate are needed for identification and verification purposes. Caste Certificate and Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate are required to determine the candidate's reservation category, with different validity periods applicable (e.g., Non-Creamy Layer valid up to 31 March 2027).

Income Certificate for 2025-26 is necessary to assess financial eligibility for scholarships. For specific categories such as SC/ST/OBC/VJ/NT, additional documents like Orphan candidate status and Minority Certificate are required. A Disability certificate from the District Civil Surgeon is mandatory for candidates with disabilities, ensuring they receive appropriate support.

The EWS (Economically Weaker Section) Certificate, valid up to 31 March 2027, is crucial for those seeking reservations under this category. Each of these documents plays a critical role in the admission process, ensuring that all students are evaluated fairly and accurately based on their qualifications and circumstances. Requirements may vary slightly depending on the specific reservation category, but generally, the core set of documents remains consistent across different categories to maintain uniformity and transparency in the admissions procedure.

source_section: admission_documents/DSY : List of Documents required for Admission and
text:
For admission to Annasaheb Dange College of Engineering and Technology in Ashta for the academic year 2025-26 under the DSY scheme, students are required to submit a comprehensive list of documents. The first document is the 10th Marksheet, which verifies the candidate's secondary education completion. Next, the 12th Marksheet ensures that the candidate has completed their higher secondary education successfully. The Diploma Marksheet is necessary for candidates who have pursued vocational training.

The Leaving / Transfer Certificate and Domicile / Nationality Certificate are essential to establish the candidate’s residential status and citizenship. The AADHAR Card (with linked mobile number) serves as a unique identification number, facilitating easier verification processes. Caste Certificates and Caste / Tribe Validity Certificate are required for identifying reservation categories such as SC/ST/OBC/VJ/NT, ensuring compliance with government policies on affirmative action.

The Non-Creamy Layer Certificate is valid until 31 March 2027, confirming that the candidate does not belong to a financially well-off category. The Income Certificate for 2025-26 provides proof of family income and can vary based on reservation categories, with different thresholds applicable to each group. For orphan candidates, an Orphan Candidate certificate is mandatory.

A Disability Certificate (Online) from the District Civil Surgeon is required for students with disabilities, ensuring they receive appropriate support during their studies. Defence Certificates are needed for candidates who have served or are serving in the armed forces. Domicile / Nationality Certificates (Father or Mother) further confirm the candidate’s residential status and citizenship.

Lastly, an EWS Certificate is necessary to identify Economically Weaker Sections, ensuring they receive specific benefits under government policies. The requirements do not significantly differ by reservation category but are essential for all candidates to ensure a fair and transparent admission process.
